evidence_text: For approximately 8 hours on March 1, 2026, Firetiger's ingest system for most customers was degraded and failed to accept OpenTelemetry data and GitHub webhooks.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "For approximately 8 hours on Sunday, March 1, 2026, Firetiger's ingest system for most customers was in a degraded state, failing to accept OpenTelemetry data and GitHub webhooks."
note:
The source describes customer impact as affecting "most customers," not all customers.
```
caveat:
The source describes customer impact as affecting "most customers," not all customers.
```

evidence_text: The postmortem attributes the outage to three overlapping issues.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "There were three overlapping issues that conspired to cause this outage."
note:
The source names three issues at a high level before detailing them later in the post.
```
caveat:
The source names three issues at a high level before detailing them later in the post.
```

evidence_text: A race condition in Firetiger's CI system, combined with aggressive concurrency control, caused a build job to be erroneously canceled during PR merge on February 27, 2026.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "A race condition in our CI systems, with excessively aggressive concurrency control, resulted in a build job getting erroneously canceled during merge of a PR on February 27, 2026."
note:
This evidence identifies the stated root failure in the CI workflow.
```
caveat:
This evidence identifies the stated root failure in the CI workflow.
```

evidence_text: A later deploy proceeded because the CI system falsely attributed build artifacts as complete and updated AWS Lambda and ECS service definitions to a non-existent container ID.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "A later deploy job falsely believed the build had completed because of a second issue in our CI system's attribution of build artifacts, and proceeded to roll out the change by updating AWS Lambda and ECS service definitions to point to a non-existent container ID."
note:
This evidence states the deployment-path failure that followed the canceled build.
```
caveat:
This evidence states the deployment-path failure that followed the canceled build.
```

evidence_text: Terraform rejected the Lambda change as invalid and aborted the deployment after ECS service definitions had already been mutated.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "This rollout was done with Terraform, which rejected the change to Lambda as invalid, aborting the deployment, but it had already mutated the ECS service definitions."
note:
The source states that deployment abort did not prevent partial mutation of ECS configuration.
```
caveat:
The source states that deployment abort did not prevent partial mutation of ECS configuration.
```

evidence_text: The invalid ECS definitions prevented new tasks from entering production, and any exiting task could not be restarted.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "ECS's definitions were thus unable to put new tasks into production, and so traffic was not shifted, but the system was in a delicate state: if any ECS tasks exited, they would not be able to be restarted because their definition was invalid."
note:
This evidence describes the service state before full customer-visible failure.
```
caveat:
This evidence describes the service state before full customer-visible failure.
```

evidence_text: Around 5:48 UTC on March 1, restarted ECS tasks failed to come up and the frontend ingest load balancers began returning 503s for all ingest events.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "About 25 hours later, around 5:48 UTC on March 1, the ECS tasks gradually did begin to get restarted, but new tasks couldn't come up, so our frontend ingest load balancers began to respond with 503s to all ingest events, including webhooks and OpenTelemetry data."
note:
The source ties visible ingest failure to task restarts approximately 25 hours after the broken deployment state was created.
```
caveat:
The source ties visible ingest failure to task restarts approximately 25 hours after the broken deployment state was created.
```

evidence_text: Firetiger's self-monitoring deployment detected the issue at 6:00, 12 minutes after the problem materialized.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "We use Firetiger (in a separate deployment) to monitor itself, so the system detected this issue at 6:00, 12 minutes after the problem materialized."
note:
The source specifies both the detection time and the elapsed detection delay.
```
caveat:
The source specifies both the detection time and the elapsed detection delay.
```

evidence_text: An integration-checking agent detected the ingest problem but initially believed it was isolated to GitHub webhook ingest.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "This agent detected and flagged the problem, believing it to be isolated to GitHub webhook ingest, and attempted (more on that) to notify human operators."
note:
The source presents this detection as partially correct but scope-limited, since the broader outage affected more than GitHub webhooks.
```
caveat:
The source presents this detection as partially correct but scope-limited, since the broader outage affected more than GitHub webhooks.
```

evidence_text: At 7:47, Firetiger's issue triaging subsystem coalesced multiple ingest-related detections and identified the bad version in ECR as the root cause.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "At 7:47, the problem got worse, as more ingest tasks restarted. Our issue triaging subsystem... identified that multiple detection agents had found problems related to ingestion... These all got coalesced into one issue, which was easily able to dig in and find the root cause, identifying the bad version in ECR."
note:
This evidence captures the source's stated root-cause identification step.
```
caveat:
This evidence captures the source's stated root-cause identification step.
```

evidence_text: Human operators were not notified for about 8 hours because an internal notification policy state hid alerts from normal incident management channels.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "Despite the good performance of our agents in finding the issue, it still took us about 8 hours to be notified of the problem." "Inadvertently, they left this in a state that hid notifications from our normal incident management channels, which resulted in the long period in which we didn't detect the issue."
note:
The source frames this as a separate internal misconfiguration that delayed human awareness despite earlier automated detection.
```
caveat:
The source frames this as a separate internal misconfiguration that delayed human awareness despite earlier automated detection.
```

evidence_text: After the issue was identified, Firetiger used Claude Code with MCP, AWS CLI, and GitHub CLI access to find the broken deployment, rebuild the image, rerun deploy scripts, and restore service health.
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents," 02 Mar 2026
source_excerpt: "Once the issue was found and we were alerted to it, we were able to quickly resolve it by pointing Claude Code at the Firetiger issue report." "Using an MCP connection, as well as AWS and Github CLI access, we were able to find the broken deployment. We rebuilt the image, re-ran deploy scripts, and returned to a healthy state."
note: This evidence covers the remediation path described in the postmortem.
caveat: This evidence covers the remediation path described in the postmortem.
